title: Intramuscular Injection
oneLiner: Intramuscular injection delivers medication into skeletal muscle for rapid systemic absorption; used when oral route is unavailable or for drugs requiring depot formulation.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Anaphylaxis** – Acute onset of urticaria, wheeze, stridor, hypotension within minutes. Single finding: **sudden drop in BP + skin flushing** after needle insertion. Stop injection immediately, call for help, give IM adrenaline 1:1000 (0.5 mL) into opposite thigh.
- **Nerve injury / haematoma** – Immediate severe radiating pain down limb, numbness, or inability to move extremity after needle insertion. Single finding: **sharp, shooting pain** that prevents further advancement. Withdraw needle, discard, re-assess site; never force. In anticoagulated patient, **rapidly expanding haematoma with neurovascular compromise** – apply firm pressure, call senior, consider surgical review.
sections.Presentation:
- **Indications:** Patient unable to take oral (vomiting, nil by mouth), need for rapid onset (e.g., antiemetic, analgesic), depot antipsychotic administration, vaccines (e.g., influenza, COVID-19, tetanus), hormones (e.g., testosterone, GnRH agonists).
- **Contraindications:** Active bleeding disorder (INR > 3, platelets < 50), local infection at site (cellulitis, abscess), muscle wasting/fibrosis at proposed site, severe coagulopathy, known allergy to injected drug.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect skin: no rash, bruising, infection, scarring at landmark.
  - Palpate muscle: adequate bulk, no tenderness.
  - Confirm landmark: deltoid (two fingerbreadths below acromion), gluteal (upper outer quadrant), vastus lateralis (middle third of thigh).
  - Check anticoagulation status – review drug chart, recent INR/platelets.
sections.Differential Diagnosis (Site Selection):
- **Most likely site:** Deltoid – for volumes ≤ 1 mL, rapid access, good vascularity. Preferred for most vaccines and small volume medications.
- **Less likely sites:**
  - Gluteal (dorsogluteal or ventrogluteal) – for volumes 1–5 mL, oily or depot preparations. Ventrogluteal preferred (safer, more consistent landmark).
  - Vastus lateralis – for children < 2 years or when other sites contraindicated (e.g., bilateral gluteal atrophy). Most reliable in infants.
- **Splitter:** Volume needed – if > 1 mL, use gluteal, not deltoid. Needle length – if patient is obese, choose longer needle (e.g., 1.5 inch) to reach muscle, else subcutaneous fat results in suboptimal absorption.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Check patient identification (name, DOB, drug allergies). Review medication chart for correct drug, dose, route, time. Inspect and palpate injection site. Aspirate before injection to exclude intravascular placement (no blood return).
- **Bloods:** Not routine. If on anticoagulation – check INR (target < 3 for most IM injections; if warfarin, [check local policy]), platelet count > 50 x 10⁹/L.
- **Imaging:** None.
- **Special tests:** None.
- **Never-miss test:** **Check for known allergies** to the drug or excipients. Review past adverse reactions (e.g., history of anaphylaxis after injection). Do not proceed without allergy confirmation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm correct patient, drug, dose, route, time, site.
  - Hand hygiene, non-sterile gloves, clean skin with alcohol swab (allow to dry 30 sec).
  - For deltoid: patient seated, arm relaxed at side; locate acromion, inject into middle of deltoid muscle (not below mid-arm).
  - For gluteal: patient lying side with knee flexed (upper leg); draw line from iliac crest to greater trochanter; inject into upper outer quadrant (avoid lower inner quadrant – sciatic nerve risk).
  - Insert needle at 90° angle with quick dart motion. Aspirate for 5–10 seconds. If blood enters syringe, withdraw, discard all, prepare fresh injection.
  - Inject slowly (1 mL/5 sec). Withdraw needle; apply sterile gauze with pressure; do not massage (can leak into subcutaneous tissue).
  - For irritant drugs (e.g., iron dextran, some antibiotics): use **Z-track technique** – pull skin and subcutaneous tissue laterally, inject, release after withdrawal.
  - Discard sharps in appropriate bin; document site (e.g., 'IM injection, right deltoid').
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor for local reaction (pain, swelling, redness) – reassure, simple analgesia (paracetamol).
  - For repeated injections (e.g., depot antipsychotics, methotrexate): rotate sites (left/right deltoid, alternate gluteal) and record on a rotation chart.
  - Check for systemic adverse effects (e.g., sedation from opioids, dystonic reaction from antipsychotics). Have flumazenil, naloxone, procyclidine available if indicated.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For depot formulations: adhere to exact frequency (weekly, monthly). Never miss or exceed interval without senior review.
  - For self-administered injections (e.g., testosterone, vitamin B12): teach patient/carer correct technique, signs of complications, and when to seek help.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not applicable for a single procedure; no routine fluids or VTE prophylaxis needed. Document injection details in notes.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (common IM drugs):**
  - Antiemetic: Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; avoid in parkinsonism).
  - Opioid analgesic: Morphine sulphate 10 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; use lower dose in elderly/opioid-naïve).
  - Benzodiazepine: Diazepam 10 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; use IV if emergency).
  - Depot antipsychotic: Haloperidol decanoate 50–200 mg every 4 weeks (**[check local protocol]**).
  - Vitamin B12: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM three times per week for 2 weeks (**[check BNF]**).
- **Common alternatives:** Cyclizine 50 mg IM (antiemetic), Pethidine 50–100 mg IM (analgesic – avoid in renal failure), Diclofenac 75 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; avoid in dehydration, CKD, history of peptic ulcer).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Anticoagulation – risk of haematoma; use smallest needle, apply pressure for 3 min.
  - Skeletal muscle relaxants (e.g., baclofen) – additive sedation with benzodiazepines/opioids.
  - Avoid IM injections in patients with neuromuscular disorders (e.g., myositis, dystrophy) – poor absorption, risk of fibrosis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Check BNF for each drug.** General: Opioids (morphine, pethidine) accumulate in renal impairment – reduce dose. Benzodiazepines (diazepam) accumulate in hepatic impairment – avoid or reduce.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Morphine – giving too high a dose (e.g., 20 mg IM) in opioid-naïve or elderly patient → respiratory depression.** Always start with lowest effective dose and have naloxone available. **Wrong route – IM instead of IV (e.g., for adrenaline) delays emergency treatment.** Use correct syringe and label.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Anaphylaxis → IM adrenaline, call crash team.
  - Local pain/bleeding/haematoma (especially with anticoagulation) → pressure, cold pack.
  - Nerve injury (sciatic, radial, axillary) → immediate dysaesthesia, muscle weakness. Stop, reassess, refer to neurology.
  - Infection (abscess, cellulitis) → from poor aseptic technique. Redness, heat, pus → surgical drainage if large, IV antibiotics.
  - Intravascular injection → systemic effects (hypotension, embolism). Aspiration before injection prevents most.
- **Chronic:**
  - Muscle fibrosis, atrophy or contracture from repeated injections at same site.
  - Lipodystrophy or granuloma (especially with depot oils).
  - Transmission of blood-borne viruses (needlestick injury) – follow local sharps protocol.
sections.Prognosis: - Excellent with correct technique; serious complications (nerve injury, abscess) occur in < 1 per 100,000 injections. Most local side effects (pain, bruising) resolve within 48–72 hours with simple analgesia.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use Z-track technique for any drug that can cause skin staining or irritation** (e.g., iron dextran, diclofenac). This prevents leakage back into subcutaneous tissue.
- **Pinch the skin for thin patients** (e.g., elderly, cachectic) to increase muscle depth and avoid injecting into bone. Use 23G/25G needle 1 inch.
- **Rotate sites meticulously** for repeated injections – document on a body chart – to avoid muscle fibrosis and ensure consistent absorption.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never inject into the lower inner quadrant of the gluteal region** – this is the area of the sciatic nerve and superior gluteal artery. Use upper outer quadrant only.
- **Do not inject into an inflamed, bruised, or scarred area** – absorption is unpredictable, pain is increased, risk of haematoma or infection rises.
- **Never inject if the patient is on therapeutic anticoagulation (INR > 3) without senior review** – risk of major haematoma. If essential, use smallest needle, compress for 5 minutes, and monitor.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** – Increasing pain, swelling, redness, or warmth at injection site (signs of infection) – **especially if fever develops**. – New numbness, tingling, or weakness in the injected limb. – Any symptoms of anaphylaxis (rash, difficulty breathing, dizziness) – go to nearest ED immediately.
- **Follow-up:** – Routine injection: no scheduled follow-up unless repeat injection needed. – Depot medication: ensure next dose is booked at correct interval.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call crash team (CBD/D or 2222) for:**
  - Anaphylaxis – stridor, hypotension, bronchospasm. Administer IM adrenaline immediately.
  - Vasovagal syncope – transient but if prolonged (>2 min) or associated with bradycardia, call for help.
- **Call senior/registrar for:**
  - Suspected nerve injury (immediate radiating pain, weakness, loss of sensation).
  - Expanding haematoma with neurovascular compromise (pain, pallor, pulselessness).
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L (rare, but septic shock from injection site infection).
- **Threshold to escalate:** GCS < 15 (not fully conscious), respiratory rate > 25, oxygen saturations < 92%, systolic BP < 90 mmHg.

rawMarkdown:
# Intramuscular Injection (IM Injection)

**One-liner:** Intramuscular injection delivers medication into skeletal muscle for rapid systemic absorption; used when oral route is unavailable or for drugs requiring depot formulation.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Anaphylaxis** – Acute onset of urticaria, wheeze, stridor, hypotension within minutes. Single finding: **sudden drop in BP + skin flushing** after needle insertion. Stop injection immediately, call for help, give IM adrenaline 1:1000 (0.5 mL) into opposite thigh.
- **Nerve injury / haematoma** – Immediate severe radiating pain down limb, numbness, or inability to move extremity after needle insertion. Single finding: **sharp, shooting pain** that prevents further advancement. Withdraw needle, discard, re-assess site; never force. In anticoagulated patient, **rapidly expanding haematoma with neurovascular compromise** – apply firm pressure, call senior, consider surgical review.

### Presentation
- **Indications:** Patient unable to take oral (vomiting, nil by mouth), need for rapid onset (e.g., antiemetic, analgesic), depot antipsychotic administration, vaccines (e.g., influenza, COVID-19, tetanus), hormones (e.g., testosterone, GnRH agonists).
- **Contraindications:** Active bleeding disorder (INR > 3, platelets < 50), local infection at site (cellulitis, abscess), muscle wasting/fibrosis at proposed site, severe coagulopathy, known allergy to injected drug.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect skin: no rash, bruising, infection, scarring at landmark.
  - Palpate muscle: adequate bulk, no tenderness.
  - Confirm landmark: deltoid (two fingerbreadths below acromion), gluteal (upper outer quadrant), vastus lateralis (middle third of thigh).
  - Check anticoagulation status – review drug chart, recent INR/platelets.

### Differential Diagnosis (Site Selection)
- **Most likely site:** Deltoid – for volumes ≤ 1 mL, rapid access, good vascularity. Preferred for most vaccines and small volume medications.
- **Less likely sites:**
  - Gluteal (dorsogluteal or ventrogluteal) – for volumes 1–5 mL, oily or depot preparations. Ventrogluteal preferred (safer, more consistent landmark).
  - Vastus lateralis – for children < 2 years or when other sites contraindicated (e.g., bilateral gluteal atrophy). Most reliable in infants.
- **Splitter:** Volume needed – if > 1 mL, use gluteal, not deltoid. Needle length – if patient is obese, choose longer needle (e.g., 1.5 inch) to reach muscle, else subcutaneous fat results in suboptimal absorption.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Check patient identification (name, DOB, drug allergies). Review medication chart for correct drug, dose, route, time. Inspect and palpate injection site. Aspirate before injection to exclude intravascular placement (no blood return).
- **Bloods:** Not routine. If on anticoagulation – check INR (target < 3 for most IM injections; if warfarin, [check local policy]), platelet count > 50 x 10⁹/L.
- **Imaging:** None.
- **Special tests:** None.
- **Never-miss test:** **Check for known allergies** to the drug or excipients. Review past adverse reactions (e.g., history of anaphylaxis after injection). Do not proceed without allergy confirmation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Confirm correct patient, drug, dose, route, time, site.
  - Hand hygiene, non-sterile gloves, clean skin with alcohol swab (allow to dry 30 sec).
  - For deltoid: patient seated, arm relaxed at side; locate acromion, inject into middle of deltoid muscle (not below mid-arm).
  - For gluteal: patient lying side with knee flexed (upper leg); draw line from iliac crest to greater trochanter; inject into upper outer quadrant (avoid lower inner quadrant – sciatic nerve risk).
  - Insert needle at 90° angle with quick dart motion. Aspirate for 5–10 seconds. If blood enters syringe, withdraw, discard all, prepare fresh injection.
  - Inject slowly (1 mL/5 sec). Withdraw needle; apply sterile gauze with pressure; do not massage (can leak into subcutaneous tissue).
  - For irritant drugs (e.g., iron dextran, some antibiotics): use **Z-track technique** – pull skin and subcutaneous tissue laterally, inject, release after withdrawal.
  - Discard sharps in appropriate bin; document site (e.g., 'IM injection, right deltoid').
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Monitor for local reaction (pain, swelling, redness) – reassure, simple analgesia (paracetamol).
  - For repeated injections (e.g., depot antipsychotics, methotrexate): rotate sites (left/right deltoid, alternate gluteal) and record on a rotation chart.
  - Check for systemic adverse effects (e.g., sedation from opioids, dystonic reaction from antipsychotics). Have flumazenil, naloxone, procyclidine available if indicated.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - For depot formulations: adhere to exact frequency (weekly, monthly). Never miss or exceed interval without senior review.
  - For self-administered injections (e.g., testosterone, vitamin B12): teach patient/carer correct technique, signs of complications, and when to seek help.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not applicable for a single procedure; no routine fluids or VTE prophylaxis needed. Document injection details in notes.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (common IM drugs):**
  - Antiemetic: Prochlorperazine 12.5 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; avoid in parkinsonism).
  - Opioid analgesic: Morphine sulphate 10 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; use lower dose in elderly/opioid-naïve).
  - Benzodiazepine: Diazepam 10 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; use IV if emergency).
  - Depot antipsychotic: Haloperidol decanoate 50–200 mg every 4 weeks (**[check local protocol]**).
  - Vitamin B12: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM three times per week for 2 weeks (**[check BNF]**).
- **Common alternatives:** Cyclizine 50 mg IM (antiemetic), Pethidine 50–100 mg IM (analgesic – avoid in renal failure), Diclofenac 75 mg IM (**[check BNF]**; avoid in dehydration, CKD, history of peptic ulcer).
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Anticoagulation – risk of haematoma; use smallest needle, apply pressure for 3 min.
  - Skeletal muscle relaxants (e.g., baclofen) – additive sedation with benzodiazepines/opioids.
  - Avoid IM injections in patients with neuromuscular disorders (e.g., myositis, dystrophy) – poor absorption, risk of fibrosis.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - **Check BNF for each drug.** General: Opioids (morphine, pethidine) accumulate in renal impairment – reduce dose. Benzodiazepines (diazepam) accumulate in hepatic impairment – avoid or reduce.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Morphine – giving too high a dose (e.g., 20 mg IM) in opioid-naïve or elderly patient → respiratory depression.** Always start with lowest effective dose and have naloxone available. **Wrong route – IM instead of IV (e.g., for adrenaline) delays emergency treatment.** Use correct syringe and label.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Anaphylaxis → IM adrenaline, call crash team.
  - Local pain/bleeding/haematoma (especially with anticoagulation) → pressure, cold pack.
  - Nerve injury (sciatic, radial, axillary) → immediate dysaesthesia, muscle weakness. Stop, reassess, refer to neurology.
  - Infection (abscess, cellulitis) → from poor aseptic technique. Redness, heat, pus → surgical drainage if large, IV antibiotics.
  - Intravascular injection → systemic effects (hypotension, embolism). Aspiration before injection prevents most.
- **Chronic:**
  - Muscle fibrosis, atrophy or contracture from repeated injections at same site.
  - Lipodystrophy or granuloma (especially with depot oils).
  - Transmission of blood-borne viruses (needlestick injury) – follow local sharps protocol.

### Prognosis
- Excellent with correct technique; serious complications (nerve injury, abscess) occur in < 1 per 100,000 injections. Most local side effects (pain, bruising) resolve within 48–72 hours with simple analgesia.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use Z-track technique for any drug that can cause skin staining or irritation** (e.g., iron dextran, diclofenac). This prevents leakage back into subcutaneous tissue.
- **Pinch the skin for thin patients** (e.g., elderly, cachectic) to increase muscle depth and avoid injecting into bone. Use 23G/25G needle 1 inch.
- **Rotate sites meticulously** for repeated injections – document on a body chart – to avoid muscle fibrosis and ensure consistent absorption.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never inject into the lower inner quadrant of the gluteal region** – this is the area of the sciatic nerve and superior gluteal artery. Use upper outer quadrant only.
- **Do not inject into an inflamed, bruised, or scarred area** – absorption is unpredictable, pain is increased, risk of haematoma or infection rises.
- **Never inject if the patient is on therapeutic anticoagulation (INR > 3) without senior review** – risk of major haematoma. If essential, use smallest needle, compress for 5 minutes, and monitor.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** – Increasing pain, swelling, redness, or warmth at injection site (signs of infection) – **especially if fever develops**. – New numbness, tingling, or weakness in the injected limb. – Any symptoms of anaphylaxis (rash, difficulty breathing, dizziness) – go to nearest ED immediately.
- **Follow-up:** – Routine injection: no scheduled follow-up unless repeat injection needed. – Depot medication: ensure next dose is booked at correct interval.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call crash team (CBD/D or 2222) for:**
  - Anaphylaxis – stridor, hypotension, bronchospasm. Administer IM adrenaline immediately.
  - Vasovagal syncope – transient but if prolonged (>2 min) or associated with bradycardia, call for help.
- **Call senior/registrar for:**
  - Suspected nerve injury (immediate radiating pain, weakness, loss of sensation).
  - Expanding haematoma with neurovascular compromise (pain, pallor, pulselessness).
  - NEWS2 > 5 or lactate > 4 mmol/L (rare, but septic shock from injection site infection).
- **Threshold to escalate:** GCS < 15 (not fully conscious), respiratory rate > 25, oxygen saturations < 92%, systolic BP < 90 mmHg.
